# crimes

> crimes is an open-source command-line tool that scans TypeScript and
> JavaScript repositories for change risk and AI-agent risk. It runs
> locally with no cloud calls, ships a stable versioned JSON contract
> so AI coding agents can consume its output safely, and is MIT-licensed.

crimes is positioned narrowly. It is not a linter (ESLint, Biome),
not a security scanner (Semgrep, CodeQL), and not a code-quality
platform (SonarQube). Its wedge is local, open-source, agent-native
codebase risk and context ‚Äî the question "where is future change most
likely to go wrong, and what should a human or AI agent know before
editing it?".

Core principles:
- Deterministic before magical. Detectors must work without any LLM.
- Evidence before judgement. Every finding cites concrete AST or git
  evidence (line ranges, churn counts, import patterns).
- JSON output is the product contract. Every report carries a
  `schema_version` and a `report_type` discriminator
  (`"scan"`, `"context"`, `"hotspots"`, `"diff"`, `"baseline"`,
  `"baseline_check"`, `"verdict"`). Breaking changes bump
  `schema_version`.
- Built for AI agents. Ships a Claude Code skill and AGENTS.md so any
  agent (Claude Code, Codex CLI, Cursor, Aider, Continue, Copilot
  Workspace) can use it without per-prompt setup.

## Install

- npm: `npm install -g crimes`
- one-shot: `npx crimes scan .`
- requires Node.js 18+

## Commands (latest published v0.6.0)

Per-file / per-directory (shipped in v0.1.0):
- `crimes scan .` ‚Äî scan repo, print top findings
- `crimes scan . --format json` ‚Äî emit the versioned JSON contract
- `crimes scan --changed --format json` ‚Äî working-tree-only post-edit scan
- `crimes context <file> --format json` ‚Äî pre-edit briefing for one file
- `crimes hotspots --since 90d --format json` ‚Äî churn √ó findings ranking

Branch / PR safety (shipped in v0.2.0):
- `crimes scan --changed --fail-on low|medium|high` ‚Äî exit 1 when the
  changed set has a finding at or above the threshold. Only valid with
  `--changed`. JSON gains `fail_on` and `failed` fields.
- `crimes diff <base...head> --format json` ‚Äî new / fixed / unchanged
  crimes between two committed refs (triple-dot range). Working-tree-safe;
  exports each ref via `git archive` into a temp dir. Findings matched
  by stable fingerprint `<type>::<file>::<symbol-or-empty>`.
- `crimes baseline save` ‚Äî snapshot current findings to
  `.crimes/baseline.json` (intended to be committed).
- `crimes baseline check --fail-on low|medium|high` ‚Äî re-scan and fail
  only on findings absent from the baseline. Default threshold `medium`.
- `crimes verdict --base origin/main --format json` ‚Äî one-line "did
  this branch make the repo cleaner, worse, unchanged, or mixed?"
  Advisory by default; opt into a hard gate with `--fail-on worse |
  new-high | new-medium`.

## CI integration (v0.2.0)

Three deterministic gating modes ‚Äî all share a uniform exit-code
contract: `0` success, `1` configured `--fail-on` threshold met,
`2` usage / environment error.

- Mode A ‚Äî `crimes scan --changed --fail-on high` (narrow,
  changed-files-only).
- Mode B ‚Äî `crimes baseline check --fail-on medium` (legacy repo;
  pin debt with a committed `.crimes/baseline.json`).
- Mode C ‚Äî `crimes verdict --base origin/main --fail-on new-high`
  (PR summary that flips to a hard gate on any new high finding).

Copy-paste GitHub Actions workflow ships in the repo at
`examples/github-actions/crimes.yml`. Full integration guide:
`docs/ci.md`.

## Structural detectors shipped today (v0.1.0)

- `large_function` (charge: God Function) ‚Äî functions over a body-line threshold
- `large_file` (charge: God File) ‚Äî files over a line threshold
- `todo_density` (charge: Unfinished Business) ‚Äî clusters of TODO/FIXME/XXX/HACK
- `direct_date` (charge: Temporal Recklessness) ‚Äî `Date.now()` / `new Date()`
  used directly in domain code

## Information architecture detectors (new in v0.3.0)

These five IA detectors are new in v0.3.0. They are deterministic:
**no LLM, no API key, and no network access** is required. Every
finding cites concrete files, line numbers, and string literals;
cross-file findings populate `related_files` and render as an "Also
touches:" block in the human report.

- `missing_agent_context` (charge: Missing Agent Context) ‚Äî repos that
  declare a `bin` in `package.json` but ship no `AGENTS.md`,
  `CLAUDE.md`, or `.claude/skills/*/SKILL.md`.
- `route_metadata_drift` (charge: Route Metadata Drift) ‚Äî route path,
  file location, default-export component name, `<title>`,
  `metadata.title`, and nav-source labels disagree for the same
  destination (‚â•3-source quorum).
- `duplicated_navigation_source` (charge: Duplicated Navigation Source)
  ‚Äî a single internal destination appears in two or more nav-like
  sources with different non-empty labels.
- `concept_alias_drift` (charge: Concept Alias Drift) ‚Äî multiple
  aliases from a seeded concept group (`team` / `workspace` / `org`;
  `plan` / `subscription` / `tier`; etc.) appear across the product
  surface, each in ‚â•2 distinct directories. Capped at the three
  strongest groups per scan.
- `docs_code_drift` (charge: Docs-Code Drift) ‚Äî a markdown doc under
  `docs/` (or a root-level `*.md`) contains a local link that does not
  resolve on disk.

IA findings phrase summaries as "appears to" / "may" ‚Äî they are
**ambiguity signals**, not claims of semantic truth. The
interpretation belongs to the human or agent reading the report. Full
reference (quorum rules, false-positive notes, suggested fixes):
`docs/finding-types/ia.md`.

Every finding (structural and IA) includes typed scores: `severity`,
`confidence`, and `agent_risk`. Default ranking is by aggregate risk,
so multiple sources of truth, weak tests, and hidden side effects can
elevate a finding even when local severity is low.

## Agent integration

- Claude Code: `.claude/skills/crimes/SKILL.md` auto-loads in any repo.
- Other agents: a top-level `AGENTS.md` is read by OpenAI Codex CLI,
  Cursor, Aider, Continue, and GitHub Copilot Workspace.

Recommended loop for any agent:
1. Before editing a file:   `crimes context <file> --format json`
2. After editing:           `crimes scan --changed --format json`
3. Before opening the PR:   `crimes verdict --base origin/main --fail-on new-high`
4. Triaging the repo:       `crimes hotspots --format json`
5. Any new `severity: "high"` finding introduced by the edit is a blocker.

## Shipped in v0.3.0 ‚Äî information architecture crimes

v0.2.0 made crimes useful for branches, PRs, CI, and agent loops.
v0.3.0 focused on detecting repo structure drift that confuses
humans, coding agents, teams, and customers ‚Äî source-of-truth and
concept drift across code, routes, navigation, copy, docs, and
ownership. The five IA detectors listed above are the headline slice.
Live on npm.

## Shipped in v0.4.0 ‚Äî agent context quality and signal-to-noise

v0.4.0 ships no new detectors. It raises the trust ratio of every
detector that already shipped. Headline additions:

- `ContextReport.related_files` ‚Äî up to ten files an agent should
  read before editing the target, ranked by a deterministic blend
  of IA-finding passthrough, shared path tokens, domain-prefix
  filename matches, and same-directory siblings. Each entry carries
  a `reason` and an ordinal `score`.
- Monorepo / nested-package root auto-detection for `crimes context`
  ‚Äî walks up to the nearest enclosing `package.json`; explicit
  `--root` still wins.
- Shape-aware `large_function` ‚Äî per-shape thresholds for `domain`,
  `test_callback`, `react_component`, `page_export`, `route_handler`,
  and `unknown` functions. React pages, route handlers, and test
  callbacks no longer trip the detector at the 60-line domain
  threshold.
- `docs_code_drift` GitHub-relative link allowlist ‚Äî `../../issues`,
  `../../pull/N`, `../../wiki/Home`, `../../blob/...` and similar
  GitHub-rewritten paths no longer flag as broken local links.
- `ScanReport.changed_files` ‚Äî `crimes scan --changed --format json`
  emits a top-level array of every file the resolver returned,
  sorted and deduplicated, including files with zero findings.
- `HotspotsReport.history_limited` (+ `history_limited_reason`) ‚Äî
  set when `git rev-parse --is-shallow-repository` returns true.
  Agents should downweight rankings when the flag is set.
- Top-level `agent_guidance` ordering in `ContextReport` ‚Äî JSON
  output places `agent_guidance` ahead of `findings`.
- Empty-field self-explanation ‚Äî `agent_guidance_reason`,
  `related_files_reason`, and `likely_tests_reason` distinguish "we
  searched and found nothing" from "we didn't search".
- `_test.ts` / `_spec.ts` likely-test discovery.

All additions are additive and backwards-compatible. `schema_version`
stays at `"0.1.0"`. Live on npm. Release notes:
https://github.com/andrewfantastic/crimes/blob/main/docs/releases/v0.4.0.md

## Detectors shipped in v0.6.0

Eighteen new detector types, grouped into four categories. All emit
the standard `Finding` shape; cross-file findings populate
`related_files`. Phrasing on the new detectors stays hedged ("appears
to" / "may") ‚Äî they are signal, not assertions of semantic truth.

Architecture / dependency graph:
- `layer_violation` (charge: Layer Border Crossing) ‚Äî an import that
  crosses a forbidden boundary defined by `architecture.layers` +
  `architecture.rules` in `crimes.config.json`.
- `circular_dependency` (charge: Tangled Imports) ‚Äî strongly-connected
  components in the import graph (‚â• 2 files).
- `deep_import` (charge: Deep Import Abuse) ‚Äî imports reaching
  more than `n` segments deep into another package's source tree.
- `high_fan_in_fan_out` (charge: Crowded Module) ‚Äî files with unusually
  high import fan-in and/or fan-out.

Information-architecture completion:
- `orphaned_destination` (charge: Orphaned Destination) ‚Äî routes
  declared in nav / IA index without a resolving source / route file.
- `parallel_destination` (charge: Parallel Destination) ‚Äî two nav-like
  surfaces declaring different routes for the same canonical destination.
- `permission_ia_drift` (charge: Permission IA Drift) ‚Äî same role /
  permission identifier categorised differently across surfaces.
- `action_label_drift` (charge: Action Label Drift) ‚Äî same domain
  action labelled differently across surfaces.
- `command_drift_docs_code_drift` (charge: Command Docs / Code Drift) ‚Äî
  docs reference a `bin` subcommand the CLI no longer implements.

Frontend / UI agent-risk:
- `design_token_escape` (charge: Design Token Escape) ‚Äî hard-coded
  colours / spacings / font sizes where the repo has a design-token
  system.
- `accessible_interaction_risk` (charge: Hidden Interaction) ‚Äî JSX
  element handles pointer events but has no accessible label.
- `duplicate_component_shape` (charge: Duplicate Component Shape) ‚Äî
  two or more components with AST-hash-equivalent JSX shape.
- `responsive_fragility` (charge: Responsive Fragility) ‚Äî component
  mixing many breakpoint-specific utilities or hard-pixel widths.
- `copy_ia_drift` (charge: Copy / IA Drift, frontend variant) ‚Äî nav
  label and breadcrumb / page-title disagree on the same destination.
- `visual_regression_review_hint` (charge: Visual Regression Review
  Hint) ‚Äî a changed line touches JSX in a way the heuristics suspect
  will alter the rendered output.

Duplication:
- `exact_duplicate_block` (charge: Exact Duplicate Block) ‚Äî function
  bodies with identical AST hashes across two or more files.
- `near_duplicate_block` (charge: Near-Duplicate Block) ‚Äî function
  bodies with high-similarity AST-hash bags but small deltas.
- `duplicated_role_status_plan_check` (charge: Duplicated Policy
  Logic) ‚Äî same domain concept (role / status / plan) checked in
  multiple files with different shapes.

Per-finding scores (M2 completion in v0.6.0):
- `scores.blast_radius` ‚Äî normalised transitive-importer count.
- `scores.churn` ‚Äî normalised commits-in-window count.
- `scores.test_gap` ‚Äî three-tier signal from filesystem + import-graph
  test discovery.
- Unified `agent_risk` formula combines all five inputs. Documented in
  `docs/scoring.md`.

Additional v0.6.0 shape: `cli_command_registrar` for `large_function`
‚Äî Commander-style `register*Command(program)` wrappers and their
anonymous `.action(...)` callbacks no longer false-positive at the
domain threshold.

Full Starlight-rendered docs at https://crimes.sh/docs/.

## Shipped in v0.5.0 ‚Äî suppressions, config, and explainability

v0.5.0 is the product-surface release: six new commands and a real
config story without any new detectors.

- `crimes init [--force]` ‚Äî bootstrap `crimes.config.json` with
  sensible defaults and inline pointers at the new knobs. Refuses to
  overwrite without `--force`. Non-interactive on purpose.
- `crimes ignore <id-or-fingerprint> --reason "‚Ä¶"` ‚Äî suppress one
  specific finding. Writes `.crimes/suppressions.json`,
  fingerprint-keyed, with a required non-empty `reason`. The file is
  intended to be committed and reviewed in PRs.
- `crimes unignore <fingerprint>` ‚Äî remove a suppression entry by
  stable fingerprint. Symmetric to `crimes ignore`; supports
  `--dry-run` and `--file <path>`. Empty `suppressions: []` is left
  in place so the file frame is visible to reviewers.
- `crimes audit-suppressions [--format human|json]` ‚Äî list every
  entry sorted oldest first, with `age_days` and per-entry concerns
  (`stale` > 180 days, `short_reason` < 16 chars, `vague_reason` for
  deferral keywords like `tmp` / `todo` / `wip` / `too noisy`).
  Emits `report_type: "audit_suppressions"`.
- `crimes explain <id-or-fingerprint> [--from <scan.json>]` ‚Äî
  deterministic long-form rationale for one finding. Prints the
  detector description, a one-paragraph `why_it_matters`, the
  finding's evidence, suggested actions, related files, and the
  verbatim `crimes ignore` command line. No LLM, no network.
- `crimes diff --fail-on new-high | new-medium` ‚Äî completes the M4
  CI-gate trio. Exits `1` when any new finding meets the threshold.
- `--show-suppressed` on `scan`, `context`, `baseline check`, `diff`,
  `verdict` ‚Äî re-surfaces matched findings annotated with
  `suppressed: true` and `suppression_reason`.

Config additions (all optional, all back-compat):

- `thresholds.largeFunction.<shape>` ‚Äî per-shape overrides for
  `domain` / `route_handler` / `react_component` / `page_export` /
  `test_callback` / `unknown`.
- `detectors.enable` / `detectors.disable` ‚Äî allowlist / blocklist on
  detector ids. Unknown ids exit `2`.
- `ia.aliasGroups` ‚Äî additive to the built-in `DEFAULT_ALIAS_GROUPS`.
- `suppressions.path` ‚Äî override the suppressions file location.
- `architecture.layers` / `architecture.rules` ‚Äî reserved
  placeholder, schema-validated but not consumed in v0.5.0.

Schema additions (all optional / additive ‚Äî **no `schema_version`
bump**):

- `Finding.suppressed` / `Finding.suppression_reason` (only present
  with `--show-suppressed`).
- `*Report.suppressed_count` on `ScanReport`, `ContextReport`,
  `BaselineCheckReport`, `DiffReport`, `VerdictReport`.
- `DiffReport.fail_on` / `DiffReport.failed` (only present with
  `--fail-on`).
- New report types: `ExplainReport` (`report_type: "explain"`),
  on-disk `Suppressions` (`report_type: "suppressions"`), and
  `AuditSuppressionsReport` (`report_type: "audit_suppressions"`).

Suppressions are applied **before** every `--fail-on` evaluation. A
suppressed finding never trips a gate, regardless of severity or
which gating command is used.

Release notes:
https://github.com/andrewfantastic/crimes/blob/main/docs/releases/v0.5.0.md

## Not shipped yet ‚Äî do not invoke or document as available

- `crimes ask` / LLM-assisted features ‚Äî v1+.
- Homebrew tap + standalone binaries ‚Äî deferred.
- Suppression expiry / owner (`expires_at`, `owner`) ‚Äî designed but deferred.
- Structured Claude + Codex evidence-capture hook ‚Äî v0.7.0.
